1: Go again, love a woman who is loved by another man and is an adulteress — the second act of the marriage drama. Whether this is Gomer returned or another woman is debated; most interpreters identify her with Gomer. The command to love a woman who is currently with another man is the enacted parable of YHWH’s love for Israel during the period of Baal apostasy: even as the LORD loves the children of Israel, though they turn to other gods. The word ʾahav (love) here is covenantal love — the same love that established the relationship is the love that pursues the unfaithful. The raisin cakes (ʾashishe anavim) were associated with Baal-Asherah festival offerings (cf. Jeremiah 7:18; Hosea 44:19), indicating Israel’s current cultic location.
2: So I bought her back for fifteen pieces of silver and a homer and a half of barley — the payment to redeem Gomer suggests she had sold herself into some form of debt-slavery or servitude to a paramour. The standard price for a slave was thirty pieces of silver (Exodus 21:32; Zechariah 11:12-13). Fifteen silver plus a homer and a half of barley (whose monetary value may equal fifteen silver) = the full slave price; or the amount reflects the degraded market value of this particular woman. The act of buying back one’s own wife who had been sold to another is both humiliating and costly — the price of covenant love when it pursues those who have abandoned it.
3: You must stay with me for many days; you are not to be a prostitute or to be with any man, and I also will be faithful to you — Hosea imposes a period of sexual exclusivity on Gomer that mirrors the waiting period Israel will experience before restoration. The phrase wĕgam-ʾanî ʾelaik (“and I also will be to you”) expresses YHWH’s reciprocal faithfulness during Israel’s period of exile and deprivation. The relationship is suspended but not dissolved; the waiting is a structured preparation for renewal rather than permanent rejection.
4: The children of Israel will live for many days without a king, without a ruler, without a sacrifice, without a sacred pillar, without an ephod or household gods — this verse describes the condition of Israel in exile and diaspora after 722 BCE with remarkable precision. Without a king: the monarchy ended with the Assyrian conquest; without a sacrifice: the temple and official cult sites were destroyed; without a sacred pillar (matstsevah): the standing stone used in both Yahwistic and Canaanite worship was removed; without an ephod: the priestly oracle device; without household gods (teraphim): the private family cult objects. The entire structure of Israelite religious and political life is dismantled — but the implied point is that this stripping away is the preparation for pure, undistracted covenant renewal.
5: Afterward the children of Israel will return and seek the LORD their God and David their king — the eschatological restoration unites the two halves of Israel’s national aspiration: covenant relationship with YHWH and Davidic kingship. David their king does not mean David personally returned, but a Davidic Messiah — the same figure Ezekiel calls “my servant David” (Ezekiel 34:23-24; Hosea 37:24-25) and Jeremiah calls “David their king” (Jeremiah 30:9). In the latter days (beʾacharit hayyamim) is the standard formula for the eschatological horizon — the fulfillment lies beyond Israel’s historical present in the messianic age.
